verses.text: The lot of the children of Joseph fell from Jordan by Jericho, unto the water of Jericho on the east, to the wilderness that goeth up from Jericho throughout mount Beth-el, goeth out from Beth-el to Luz, passeth along unto the borders of Archi to Ataroth, goeth down westward to the coast of Japhleti, unto the coast of Beth-horon the nether, and to Gezer: the goings out thereof are at the sea. Guidance: Joseph's lot — covering both his sons — runs from the Jordan to the Mediterranean. The line passes Beth-el (Jacob's stair-of-heaven), Luz (Jacob's first night out from Esau's threat), and Beth-horon (where God rained hailstones at Gibeon). The map reads like a memoir. Joseph's grandsons inherit the very horizon his grandfather wrestled across.

Joseph's grandsons inherit the very horizon his grandfather wrestled across.

So the children of Joseph, Manasseh and Ephraim, took their inheritance. Guidance: One sentence, two tribes. Joseph, who had been sold to Egypt for twenty pieces of silver, is here counted as two tribes among Israel — Genesis 48:5 has come to its fulfillment. The brother taken away from the family ends up multiplied in it. God is patient bookkeeper: what looks like a loss to a teenager in a pit reads like a double portion to his great-grandchildren in their fields.

The border of the children of Ephraim by their families was thus: the border of their inheritance on the east side was Ataroth-addar, unto Beth-horon the upper; the border went out toward the sea to Michmethah on the north; the border turned eastward unto Taanath-shiloh, passed by it on the east to Janohah; went down from Janohah to Ataroth, and to Naarath, came to Jericho, went out at Jordan. From Tappuah westward unto the river Kanah; the goings out thereof were at the sea. This is the inheritance of the tribe of the children of Ephraim by their families. The separate cities for the children of Ephraim were among the inheritance of the children of Manasseh, all the cities with their villages. Guidance: Ephraim, the younger son whom Jacob blessed with the right hand (Gen 48:14), gets a heart-of-the-country portion. Shiloh — where the tabernacle will rest for centuries — sits in Ephraim. Joshua himself is an Ephraimite. The verse mentions 'separate cities' for Ephraim inside Manasseh's area: the younger brother literally has homes in the elder brother's territory. Family is not always clean lines. God writes his deeds with grace overlapping property.

They drave not out the Canaanites that dwelt in Gezer: but the Canaanites dwell among the Ephraimites unto this day, and serve under tribute. Guidance: The second 'unevicted' note in three chapters. Gezer was given by name in Joshua 10:33; here it remains Canaanite. The compromise is monetized — they pay tribute, so they stay. Money replaces obedience. Eventually Solomon will buy Gezer through marriage diplomacy with Pharaoh's daughter (1 Kings 9:16), but the spiritual damage will take generations to undo. Half-finished obedience reaches you as a tax someone else collects from your inheritance.
